slides.title: Visual Adversarial Attack on Vision-Language Models for Autonomous Driving
slides.subtitle: Paper summary: ADvLM — Tianyuan Zhang et al. (arXiv)

steps.heading: What this is
steps.body_markdown: A concise presentation of ADvLM, a visual adversarial attack framework tailored to Vision-Language Models (VLMs) used in autonomous driving (AD). See source paper for figures and full details. (PDF pages: 1–2).

slides.title: Motivation & Risk
slides.subtitle: Why attack VLMs in AD matters

steps.heading: VLMs in AD
steps.body_markdown: VLMs combine visual inputs and natural language reasoning for perception and planning in AD — useful but safety-critical. (Intro; page 1).

steps.heading: Security concern
steps.body_markdown: Adversarial visual perturbations can change VLM outputs (wrong actions), producing dangerous vehicle behavior (illustrated in Fig.1, page 1).

slides.title: Two unique AD attack challenges
slides.subtitle: What makes AD different from generic VLM attacks

steps.heading: Textual variability
steps.body_markdown: The same instruction can be phrased many ways (e.g., “turn left” vs “turn left ahead”). Attacks must work across semantically equivalent prompts. (Sec.3; page 2).

steps.heading: Time-series visual scenarios
steps.body_markdown: Driving inputs are frame sequences with changing viewpoints; perturbations must generalize across selected pivotal frames. (Sec.3; Fig.2, page 3).

steps.heading: Semantic-Invariant Induction (text)
steps.body_markdown: Generate a low-semantic-entropy (LSE) prompt library: use an LLM (e.g., GPT-4V) to create diverse yet semantically consistent prompt variants to cover textual variability. (Sec.4.1; page 4).

steps.heading: Scenario-Associated Enhancement (vision)
steps.body_markdown: Select pivotal frames via attention analysis and optimize perturbations to generalize across viewpoints and frames. Combined loss balances image-wise and scene-wise objectives. (Sec.4.2; Eqns.6–10; page 4–5).

steps.heading: Generate and score variants
steps.body_markdown: Use an LLM to propose prompt variants. Compute a penalty B(t, t_i)=SE(t_i)+β·D(t,t_i) combining semantic entropy and expression distance; select low-entropy, diverse prompts to build LSE library. (Sec.4.1; Eqns.3–5).

steps.heading: Why it helps
steps.body_markdown: Optimizing perturbations over multiple semantically equivalent prompts makes attacks robust to wording changes in AD instructions.

steps.heading: Pivotal frame selection
steps.body_markdown: Iteratively choose frames that maximize attention-map diversity (minimize similarity to already-selected frames), producing a representative set Ve for optimization. (Sec.4.2; Eqns.7–8; page 5).

steps.heading: Loss functions
steps.body_markdown: Limage enforces effectiveness over perspective transforms; Lscene enforces sequence generalization. Final attack loss: Lattack = (1−λ)·Limage + λ·Lscene. (Eqn.10; page 5).

steps.heading: Setup
steps.body_markdown: Evaluated on AD VLMs (DriveLM, Dolphins, LMDrive) and general VLMs; open-loop and closed-loop (CARLA) tests; white-box and black-box settings. (Sec.5; pages 5–7).

steps.heading: Main findings
steps.body_markdown: ADvLM yields the strongest attack effectiveness: e.g., Final Score drops (DriveLM) up to ~16.97% in white-box; closed-loop shows increased collisions (Table 1–2, pages 6–7). Prefetch ablations show 3 prompts and |Ve|≈6 are effective. (Tables & figures pages 6–8).

steps.heading: Real-world vehicle test
steps.body_markdown: Physical vehicle trials show ADvLM can cause route deviations in many trials (70% deviation in reported experiment). (Sec.6; page 8).

steps.heading: Limitations & future work
steps.body_markdown: Authors note future directions: universal attacks, targeted attacks, broader multimodal settings. Emphasize need for defenses in safety-critical systems. (Sec.7; page 9).
